labs.title: IAM Root Account Security
labs.description: Secure your AWS root account with best practices.

steps.text: Login to AWS using root account.
steps.description: Navigate to the [AWS Management Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/) and sign in using your root email address and password.
In the search bar at the top of the console, type **IAM** and select it from the services list to open the Identity and Access Management dashboard.

Under **Security recommendations**, click on **Add MFA**. Choose your device type (e.g., Virtual authenticator app like Authy or Google Authenticator) and follow the on-screen prompts to scan the QR code and sync it.
Go to **Account settings** in the left navigation pane. Click **Edit** under Password policy to enforce minimum password length, require symbols/numbers, and enable password expiration.

steps.text: Avoid using root account for daily work.
steps.description: The root account has unrestricted access. Best practice is to create a separate IAM user with **AdministratorAccess** for daily tasks, log out of the root account, and store the root credentials securely.

labs.title: Create IAM User
labs.description: Create IAM users for secure access.

steps.description: From the IAM dashboard, click on **Users** under the Access management section in the left-hand navigation pane.
Click the blue **Create user** button located in the top right corner of the Users panel.
In the User details section, type `dev-user` in the **User name** input field.

Check the box for **Provide user access to the AWS Management Console**. Select **I want to create an IAM user** and choose either an autogenerated password or a custom password. *Tip: Uncheck 'User must create a new password' if this is just for testing.*

Click **Next**, assign any immediate permissions if needed, review the details, and click **Create user**. **Crucial:** Download the `.csv` file containing the login credentials before leaving the page.
In the IAM dashboard, select **User groups** from the left-hand navigation menu.
Click the blue **Create group** button at the top right.
Under User group name, enter `Developers`.
Scroll down to **Attach permissions policies**. Search for and check the box next to `AmazonEC2FullAccess` and `AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess`.
Scroll back up to the **Add users to the group** section, select `dev-user`, and finally click **Create group** at the bottom of the page.
Navigate to IAM **Users** and click on the name of the user you wish to modify (e.g., `dev-user`).
In the Permissions tab, click the **Add permissions** dropdown and select **Add permissions**.
Select **Attach policies directly**. Search for `ReadOnlyAccess`, check the box next to it, and click **Next** then **Add permissions**.
Log in as the newly modified user. Try to view an EC2 instance (should succeed) and then try to terminate one (should fail with an access denied error).
Search for **EC2** in the AWS console search bar and open the service.
Click the orange **Launch instance** button. Give your instance a name tag, like `MyFirstWebserver`.
Under **Application and OS Images (Amazon Machine Image)**, ensure the `Amazon Linux 2023 AMI` is selected. This is Free Tier eligible.
Scroll to **Instance type** and ensure `t2.micro` is selected (or `t3.micro` depending on your region's Free Tier).
Under **Key pair (login)**, select an existing key pair or click **Create new key pair**. Leave network settings as default for now, and click **Launch instance**.
Go to **Instances** in the EC2 dashboard. Wait until your instance state shows as `Running` and status checks have passed.
Select the checkbox next to your instance and click the **Connect** button at the top of the dashboard.
Ensure the **EC2 Instance Connect** tab is selected. Leave the default username as `ec2-user` and click **Connect**. A new browser tab with a terminal will open.

Verify your connection by running simple Linux commands:
```bash
whoami
pwd
cat /etc/os-release
```

Inside your EC2 terminal, apply the latest security updates and bug fixes:
```bash
sudo yum update -y
```

Install the Apache web server package:
```bash
sudo yum install httpd -y
```

Start the web server and enable it to start automatically on reboot: 
```bash
sudo systemctl enable httpd
sudo systemctl start httpd
```
Go back to the AWS Console, copy your instance's **Public IPv4 address**, and paste it into a new browser tab. Note: If it times out, you need to configure your Security Group (Next Lab).
In the EC2 dashboard left-hand menu, scroll down to **Network & Security** and click **Security Groups**.
Select the security group attached to your instance. Click the **Inbound rules** tab, then **Edit inbound rules**. Click **Add rule**, set Type to `HTTP`, and Source to `Anywhere-IPv4` (`0.0.0.0/0`).
Ensure there is an existing rule with Type `SSH`, Port `22`. For better security, set the Source to `My IP` so only your network can SSH into the server.
Click **Save rules**. Refresh the browser tab where you tried to access your web server's public IP. It should now display your webpage instantly.
Search for **S3** in the AWS console and open the Simple Storage Service dashboard.
Click the orange **Create bucket** button on the right side of the screen.
Bucket names must be globally unique across all AWS accounts. Enter a name like `my-data-bucket-lastname-date`. Leave the Region as default.
Scroll down to the bottom, leaving all other settings (like Block Public Access) at their default values for security, and click **Create bucket**.
Click on the name of your newly created bucket from the S3 Buckets list.
In the Objects tab, click the orange **Upload** button.
Click **Add files** or drag and drop a test file (like a `.txt` or `.jpg` image) from your computer into the upload area.
Scroll down and click **Upload**. Once it shows as 'Succeeded', click **Close** to see your file listed in the bucket.
Navigate into your target S3 bucket from the S3 dashboard.
Click on the **Properties** tab located right below the bucket name.
Find the **Bucket Versioning** section and click **Edit**. Select **Enable** and click **Save changes**.
Create a local file `test.txt` with the text 'Version 1'. Upload it. Then change the local file text to 'Version 2' and upload it again to the exact same bucket.
In the **Objects** tab, toggle the **Show versions** switch to ON. You will now see both iterations of `test.txt` with unique Version IDs.
In your bucket, go to **Properties**. Scroll to the very bottom to **Static website hosting**. Click **Edit**, select **Enable**, set Index document to `index.html`, and save.
Create a simple HTML file named `index.html` on your PC. Go to the **Objects** tab and upload this file to your bucket.
Go to the **Permissions** tab. Under **Block public access (bucket settings)**, click **Edit**, uncheck **Block all public access**, type `confirm` in the modal, and save.
Go back to **Properties**, scroll down to **Static website hosting**, and click the generated **Bucket website endpoint** URL to view your live site.
Search for **VPC** in the AWS console and open it.
Click the orange **Create VPC** button. Choose **VPC only** for this manual setup lab.
Name your VPC (e.g., `MyCustomVPC`). In the IPv4 CIDR block field, enter `10.0.0.0/16`. This provides 65,536 private IP addresses.
Leave IPv6 and Tenancy as default. Click **Create VPC** at the bottom right.
In the left-hand VPC navigation pane, click on **Subnets**.
Click **Create subnet**. In the dropdown, select the VPC you just created (`MyCustomVPC`).
Name the subnet `PublicSubnet1`. Choose the first available Availability Zone (e.g., `us-east-1a`).
Enter `10.0.1.0/24` as the IPv4 CIDR block (provides 256 IPs). Click **Create subnet**.
Go to **Internet Gateways** in the left menu. Click **Create internet gateway**, name it `MyIGW`, and click Create.
Select the newly created IGW, click the **Actions** dropdown, and select **Attach to VPC**. Select your `MyCustomVPC` and attach it.
To make the subnet truly public, we must update the route table. Proceed to the next lab to complete this critical step.
Once routes are configured, instances launched in this subnet with a public IP will be able to ping external networks like `google.com`.
Navigate to **Route Tables** in the VPC dashboard. Identify the route table associated with your `MyCustomVPC`.
Select the route table, navigate to the **Routes** tab at the bottom, and click **Edit routes**.
Click **Add route**. Under Destination, enter `0.0.0.0/0`. Under Target, select **Internet Gateway** and choose `MyIGW`. Click **Save changes**.
Switch to the **Subnet associations** tab. Click **Edit subnet associations**, check the box next to `PublicSubnet1`, and click **Save associations**. Your subnet is now officially a Public Subnet.
In the EC2 Dashboard, scroll down the left menu to **Load Balancing** and click **Load Balancers**.
Click **Create Load Balancer**. Choose **Application Load Balancer** (ALB). Name it `MyWebALB`. Ensure it is set to Internet-facing and select at least two Availability Zones in your VPC.
Under Listeners and routing, ensure Protocol is set to `HTTP` and Port is `80`.
Under the Default action, click **Create target group**. A new tab will open. Choose **Instances** as target type, name it `MyWebTargets`, protocol HTTP port 80, and click Next to create it.
Return to the Target Groups page and click on your newly created `MyWebTargets` group.
Go to the **Targets** tab and click **Register targets**. Select your running web server EC2 instances, click **Include as pending below**, and then **Register pending targets**.
Wait a few minutes. The Health status of your targets should transition from `Initial` to `Healthy`. If it fails, check your EC2 security groups.
Go back to your Load Balancer summary page. Copy the **DNS name** (e.g., `mywebalb-1234.elb.amazonaws.com`) and paste it into a browser. You should see your website.
In the EC2 dashboard under Instances, click **Launch Templates**.
Click **Create launch template**. Give it a name like `WebAppTemplate` and provide a description.
Select `Amazon Linux 2023 AMI`, choose `t2.micro` for Instance Type, and select your Key Pair. Under Security Groups, choose your Web Server security group.
Scroll down to Advanced details. Under **User data**, paste your bash script to install Apache (from the EC2 lab). Click **Create launch template**.
In the EC2 Dashboard, under Auto Scaling, click **Auto Scaling Groups** and click Create.
Name the group `MyWebASG`. Select the `WebAppTemplate` you just created and click Next. Choose your VPC and multiple subnets.
Under Group size, set Desired capacity to `2`, Minimum capacity to `1`, and Maximum capacity to `4`. For scaling policies, choose Target tracking scaling policy based on CPU utilization at 50%.
Under Load balancing, choose **Attach to an existing load balancer**. Select your ALB's Target Group so new instances automatically receive traffic. Click Next until you create the ASG.
Search for **RDS** in the AWS console and open the Relational Database Service.
Click the **Create database** button. Choose **Standard create**.
Select **MySQL** as the engine type. Under Templates, choose **Free tier** to avoid unexpected charges.
Name your DB instance identifier (e.g., `mydb`). Set the Master username to `admin` and generate a strong Master password.
Under Connectivity, set **Public access** to Yes (for lab purposes only). Ensure your VPC security group allows port `3306`. Click **Create database**. This will take several minutes.
Once the DB status is 'Available', click on it and copy the **Endpoint** URL from the Connectivity & security tab.
Download and open a local SQL client like MySQL Workbench, DBeaver, or DataGrip.
Create a new connection. Paste the Endpoint as the Host. Use Port `3306`, Username `admin`, and enter your master password. Click Test Connection.
Go back to the RDS Dashboard and click on **Databases**. Select your `mydb` instance.
Click the **Actions** dropdown menu at the top right and select **Take snapshot**.
Enter a descriptive Snapshot name, for example: `mydb-manual-backup-v1`.
Click **Take snapshot**. You can view the progress in the **Snapshots** menu on the left. Once available, you can restore a full database from this image.
Search for **DynamoDB** in the AWS console and open the service.
Click the orange **Create table** button.
Enter `UsersTable` for the Table name. Enter `UserId` as the Partition key and set the data type to `String`.
Leave the table settings as Default. Scroll to the bottom and click **Create table**. It will provision almost instantly.
From the Tables list, click on `UsersTable`.
Click the **Explore table items** button located in the top right corner.
Click **Create item**. Enter a value for UserId (e.g., `user-001`). Click **Add new attribute**, choose String, name it `FullName`, and give it a value (e.g., `Jane Doe`).
Click **Create item**. You will now see your JSON-like record displayed in the Items returned list.
Search for **Lambda** in the AWS console and open the service.
Click **Create function**. Choose the **Author from scratch** option.
Name your function `HelloWorldFunction`. Under Runtime, select **Python 3.12** (or the latest available Python version). Leave architecture as x86_64.
Leave default execution role settings (it will create a basic Lambda permissions role) and click **Create function**.
In the function overview, scroll down to the Code Source editor. Click the dropdown arrow next to **Test** and select **Configure test event**. Name it `TestEvent` and click Save.
Click the blue **Test** button to manually trigger your Lambda function.
A green box will appear showing execution results. Expand the **Details** to see the response payload
Click the **Monitor** tab and then **View CloudWatch logs** to see memory usage, duration, and any `print()` statements from your code.
Go to the S3 Dashboard and click on the bucket you created in the previous S3 lab.
Go to the **Properties** tab. Scroll down to **Event notifications** and click **Create event notification**. Name it `TriggerLambda`.
Under Event types, check **All object create events**. Scroll to Destination, select **Lambda Function**, choose your `HelloWorldFunction`, and Save.
Upload any file to the S3 bucket. Then go to your Lambda function's CloudWatch logs to verify it was automatically invoked by the S3 event.
Search for **API Gateway** in the AWS console.
Scroll to **REST API** (not the Private one) and click **Build**. Choose **New API**, name it `MyDemoAPI`, and click **Create API**.
Click **Create resource**, name it `hello`, and click Create. Then select the `/hello` resource, click **Create method**, choose **GET**, and set Integration type to Mock (for now).
Click the **Deploy API** button. Create a new stage named `dev` and deploy. You will be given an Invoke URL.
Go back to your `MyDemoAPI` resources. Select the GET method under `/hello` and click **Integration Request**.
Edit the integration type. Change it from Mock to **Lambda Function**. Check the box for Use Lambda Proxy integration.
Search for your `HelloWorldFunction` in the Lambda function field. Save and grant API Gateway permission to invoke your function.
Deploy the API to the `dev` stage again. Copy the Invoke URL, append `/hello` to the end, and paste it in your browser. You should see `"Hello from Lambda!"`.
Search for **CloudWatch** in the AWS console and open it.
In the left menu, go to **Metrics** -> **All metrics**. Select **EC2** -> **Per-Instance Metrics**. Find your EC2 instance and check the box for `CPUUtilization`.
Click the **Graphed metrics** tab, and click the bell icon (**Create alarm**) next to CPUUtilization.
Set the threshold condition to `Greater/Equal` than `80` percent. Click Next, configure an SNS topic to send an email alert to yourself, and create the alarm.

labs.title: Enable CloudTrail
labs.description: Track all user activities in AWS.

steps.description: Search for **CloudTrail** in the console and navigate to the dashboard.
Click the **Create trail** button. Name it `ManagementEventsTrail`.
Ensure it is configured as a Multi-region trail so that it logs API calls regardless of where they happen.
Under Storage location, choose to create a new S3 bucket. AWS will automatically generate a bucket policy to allow CloudTrail to write logs there.
Go to the **Event history** tab. You can filter and view recent API calls made in your account (e.g., RunInstances, ConsoleLogin).
Search for Key Management Service (**KMS**) in the AWS console.
Click **Create key**. Keep it Symmetric. Give it an Alias like `MyDataKey`.
Define Key Administrators (who can manage the key) and Key Usage Permissions (who can encrypt/decrypt with it). Add your `dev-user`.
You can now select this custom KMS key when launching an encrypted EBS volume or an RDS instance.
Navigate to the S3 Dashboard and open an existing bucket.
Click on the **Properties** tab.
Scroll down to **Default encryption**. By default, it is Server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys (SSE-S3). Click **Edit**.
Change the encryption type to **SSE-KMS** and choose the KMS key you created in the previous lab. Click **Save changes**.
Search for Simple Notification Service (**SNS**) in the AWS console.
On the left menu, select **Topics** and click **Create topic**. Choose **Standard** type, and name it `AlertTopic`.
Once created, click **Create subscription**. Select Protocol as `Email` and enter your personal email address as the Endpoint.
Check your email inbox. You will receive an 'AWS Notification - Subscription Confirmation' email. Click the **Confirm subscription** link.
Return to your `AlertTopic` in the SNS console.
Click the **Publish message** button in the top right corner.
Add a Subject like `Test Alert`. In the Message body, write `This is a test notification from AWS SNS.`
Scroll down and click **Publish message**. Check your email to see the incoming message.
Search for Simple Queue Service (**SQS**) in the AWS console.
Click **Create queue**. Leave the type as **Standard**. Name it `JobQueue` and click Create.
Click **Send and receive messages**. In the Message body, type `Process job ID 1234` and click **Send message**.
Scroll down to the Receive messages section and click **Poll for messages**. Click on the message ID to view the body data you sent.
Search for **CloudFront** in the AWS console.
Click the **Create Distribution** button.
In the Origin domain dropdown, select the S3 bucket where you hosted your static website. Select **Origin access control settings (recommended)** to keep the S3 bucket private while CF accesses it.
Set Default root object to `index.html`. Click **Create distribution**. Note: You will need to copy the generated S3 Bucket Policy and apply it in S3.
Wait for the deployment status to change from 'Deploying' to a timestamp. Copy the **Distribution domain name** (e.g., `d1234.cloudfront.net`) and paste it in a browser.
Click on your account name in the top right corner and select **Billing and Cost Management**.
Go to **Billing Preferences** on the left. Check **Receive Billing Alerts** and save preferences.
Go to CloudWatch -> Alarms -> Create Alarm. Select Metric: **Billing** -> **Total Estimated Charge** -> USD.
Set the threshold condition to Greater than `$10.00`. Set up an SNS topic to email you if charges exceed this amount.
In the Billing Dashboard, click **Cost Explorer** on the left menu. Click **Launch Cost Explorer**.
Review the default daily unblended cost graph to see your spending trends over the past month.
On the right panel, under **Group by**, select **Service**. The bar chart will recolor to show exactly which AWS services are costing you money.
Change the date range and granularity (e.g., Monthly). This helps predict future bills and locate resources you may have forgotten to terminate.
Create a new private S3 bucket. Ensure 'Block all public access' remains checked.
Upload your HTML, CSS, and JS files. Make sure the main entry file is named `index.html`.
Go to bucket Properties and enable Static Website Hosting, specifying `index.html` as the root document.
Create a CloudFront distribution pointing to the S3 bucket. Use Origin Access Control (OAC) to generate a secure bucket policy. Apply that policy in S3.
Wait for CF to deploy. Access your fully secure, cached, and globally distributed static website via the `.cloudfront.net` URL.

labs.title: Scalable Web App
labs.description: Deploy scalable app using EC2 + ALB + ASG.

steps.description: Create a Launch Template with Amazon Linux, install a web server via user data, and attach a security group allowing HTTP port 80.
Create an Application Load Balancer across at least 2 Availability Zones. Create a Target Group.
Ensure the Load Balancer routes port 80 traffic to your Target Group.
Create an ASG using your Launch Template. Set Desired 2, Min 2, Max 4. Attach it to your ALB's Target Group.

SSH into one EC2 instance and run a stress test (e.g., `sudo amazon-linux-extras install epel -y && sudo yum install stress -y && stress --cpu 8`). Watch Auto Scaling spin up a new instance in response.
Create a Python Lambda function that reads or writes data. Give its IAM role permissions to access DynamoDB.
Create a REST API. Add a resource (e.g., `/data`) and create a POST or GET method.
Set the API method integration to Lambda Proxy Integration and point it to your Python function.
Deploy your API Gateway to a stage (e.g., `prod`) and copy the Invoke URL.
Use an API testing tool like Postman or `curl` to send a request to your API Gateway URL and see the Lambda execute.
Open your EC2 console and navigate to running instances.
Select an EC2 instance that you have already configured (e.g., one with a web server and custom HTML installed).
Click **Actions** -> **Image and templates** -> **Create image**. Name it `MyCustomWebAMI` and click Create.
Go to **AMIs** on the left menu. Select your new image and click **Launch instance from AMI**. It will contain all your installed software.
In the EC2 dashboard, scroll down the left menu to **Network & Security** -> **Elastic IPs**.
Click **Allocate Elastic IP address** and click **Allocate**.
Select the newly created IP. Click **Actions** -> **Associate Elastic IP address**. Select your target EC2 instance and click Associate.
Your instance now has a static IP that will not change if the instance stops and starts. Test SSH or HTTP using this new Elastic IP.
Under Elastic Block Store on the left menu, select **Volumes**.
Click **Create volume**. Choose `gp3`, set size to 5GB, and importantly, ensure it is created in the **exact same Availability Zone** as your running EC2 instance.
Select the new volume. Click **Actions** -> **Attach volume**. Select your instance and click Attach.
SSH into your EC2 instance. Format the volume: `sudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/xvdf`. Create a mount point: `sudo mkdir /data`. Mount it: `sudo mount /dev/xvdf /data`.
In the **Volumes** panel, select a volume, click **Actions** -> **Create snapshot**. Give it a description and click Create.
Go to **Snapshots** in the left menu. Wait until the snapshot Status changes from `pending` to `completed`.
Select the completed snapshot. Click **Actions** -> **Create volume from snapshot**. Choose an Availability Zone and click Create.
You can now attach this newly restored volume to any EC2 instance in the matching Availability Zone to recover the data.
Search for **CloudFormation** in the AWS console.
Click **Create stack** -> **With new resources (standard)**.

Select **Upload a template file**. Create a local `.yaml` file to deploy an S3 bucket:
```yaml
Resources:
  MyBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
```
Upload this file.
Name the stack `MyS3Stack`, click Next, and click **Submit**. Watch the events tab as CloudFormation automates the creation of the bucket.
In CloudFormation, select your `MyS3Stack`.
Click the **Update** button at the top right.

Select **Replace current template**. Update your local YAML file to enable versioning:
```yaml
Resources:
  MyBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      VersioningConfiguration:
        Status: Enabled
```
Upload the new file.
Review the 'Change set' preview to see what CloudFormation will modify. Click **Submit** to apply the configuration update.
Search for **Elastic Beanstalk** in the AWS console.
Click **Create application**. Give it an Application Name like `MyNodeApp`. Under Platform, choose `Node.js`.
Under Application code, choose **Sample application** to use AWS's default test app, or upload a ZIP file of your own code.
Click **Create application**. Elastic Beanstalk will automatically provision the EC2 instances, security groups, load balancers, and deploy your code. Click the generated environment URL to view it.
Ensure your EC2 instance has an IAM role with `CloudWatchAgentServerPolicy` attached.
Install the AWS CLI on your EC2 instance or use AWS CloudShell to send metric data.

Run the following AWS CLI command to send a custom memory metric:
```bash
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data --metric-name MemoryUsage --namespace CustomSpace --value 75
```
Go to CloudWatch -> Metrics -> All metrics. Look for the `CustomSpace` namespace, select `MemoryUsage`, and view your custom data point on the graph.
Launch WebServer1 in Subnet A (us-east-1a) and WebServer2 in Subnet B (us-east-1b). Ensure both have web servers running.
Create an Application Load Balancer and ensure its Target Group includes both instances.
Access the ALB DNS URL. Refresh a few times to see traffic balance. Go to EC2 and manually **Stop** WebServer1.
Refresh the ALB DNS URL. Your website stays online because the Load Balancer detects WebServer1 is unhealthy and routes 100% of traffic to WebServer2 in the other AZ.
Create a Node.js or Python Lambda function that writes an item to DynamoDB. Give it the `AmazonDynamoDBFullAccess` IAM policy.
Create a REST API Gateway with a POST method. Connect it to your Lambda function as a proxy.
Create a DynamoDB table named `Requests` with Partition key `RequestId`.
Make an HTTP POST request to your API Gateway URL. Verify that the request correctly executed the Lambda function and successfully saved a record in DynamoDB.
